### Key Findings (Well-Supported)

- Synaptic plasticity defects
- Neuronal hyperactivity

- Tau phosphorylation and ER stress

# Literature-Anchored Debrief: RyR2 Calcium Signaling in Alzheimer's Disease

## Executive Summary

Research on ryanodine receptor type 2 (RyR2) calcium channels has emerged as a compelling mechanistic pathway in Alzheimer's disease (AD) pathogenesis. The central hypothesis posits that post-translational remodeling of RyR2 causes calcium "leak" from the endoplasmic reticulum (ER), triggering downstream signaling defects that lead to synaptic dysfunction, cognitive decline, and amyloid-beta accumulation. Two primary papers anchor this literature: Bussiere et al. (2017) establishing the RyR2 leak mechanism [1], and Zundock et al. (2023) elucidating the autophagy rescue pathway [2]. A third key paper demonstrates that limiting RyR2 open time pharmacologically or genetically prevents AD-related neuronal hyperactivity [3].
